title: Fitting a mortise lock and euro-cylinder
subtitle: Mark the mortise, chisel it out, set the cylinder, fit the furniture
excerpt: How to fit a 5-lever mortise deadlock and euro-cylinder to an internal or external door: marking and chiselling the mortise, fitting the latch case, drilling for the cylinder, fitting the cylinder and rose, and cutting the strike plate into the frame.
sourceNotes: British Standards BS 3621 (thief-resistant mortise locks). Collins Complete DIY Manual. NHBC Standards (publicly available, external door security sections).

content.text: is a two-part job: fitting the lock body into the door edge, and cutting the

into the door frame. The lock body is chiselled and drilled into the edge of the door; the strike plate is recessed into the frame so the bolt extends into the pocket when the door is closed. For external doors, a 5-lever BS 3621-rated mortise lock meets most home insurance minimum requirements.
Step 1, position the lock case on the door edge.

Hold the lock case on the door edge at the intended height (typically 1,000 mm above the floor to the centre line of the cylinder). Mark the top and bottom of the case and the depth of the case front plate (the forend) on the door edge. Mark the centreline of the cylinder and keyhole on the door face.

Drill a series of overlapping holes along the mortise pocket with the cordless drill fitted with a flat bit or Forstner bit matching the lock-case width. Drill to the depth of the lock case. Keep the drill at 90 degrees to the door edge.

Chop the waste between the drilled holes with the 9 mm mortise chisel, working down to the pencilled depth. The mortise sides must be clean and vertical; the lock case should slide in without rocking. Test the fit before recessing the forend.

With the lock case in the mortise, mark around the forend plate with a marking knife. Chisel a shallow recess (the depth of the plate) so the forend sits flush with the door edge. A forend that stands proud prevents the door closing tight against the frame.

Using a flat bit or Forstner bit at the diameter specified by the lock (typically 33 or 35 mm), drill through the door face from both sides at the marked cylinder centreline. Drilling from both sides prevents breakout on the exit face.

through the door face. The cam (the metal tongue at the back) must engage with the lock mechanism. Fix the cylinder with the securing screw provided through the lock case. Fit the door handles and rosette plates.

Close the door until the bolt touches the frame. The bolt face will leave a mark on the frame, or rub chalk onto the bolt face and close the door for a chalk impression on the frame. Mark the bolt outline on the frame.

Hold the strike plate over the bolt mark and trace around it. Chisel the plate recess to the plate thickness. Drill out the bolt pocket at the marked bolt position. Screw the plate into the recess. Test the door: the bolt should extend fully into the pocket when the key is turned.

attrs.fix: Elongate the pocket in the frame slightly with a chisel. If the misalignment is significant, reposition the strike plate and fill and re-drill the fixing holes.
attrs.cause: Strike plate is misaligned vertically or horizontally.
attrs.symptom: Bolt does not enter the strike pocket cleanly

attrs.fix: Measure the door thickness and select the correct cylinder size. The cylinder should protrude no more than 3 mm beyond the door face on the key side.
attrs.cause: Cylinder is too long for the door thickness and the securing screw is overtightened, pressing on the cylinder cam.
